answer:
\boxed{C}

The **sector model** (proposed by Homer Hoyt) suggests that urban areas develop in wedge-shaped sectors radiating outward from the central business district (CBD), with residential and commercial zones following transportation corridors. Unlike the concentric zone model, it acknowledges that **higher-income groups tend to live farther from the CBD** along these sectors, while lower-income groups occupy areas closer to the center. This reflects a more nuanced spatial distribution than rigid zoning or radial growth patterns.
